coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence of carbon dots from mesoporous silica
coredata.prism:issueName: 5-th International Workshop on Advanced Spectroscopy and Optical Materials (IWASOM’15)
coredata.dc:creator: Nelson, D.K.
Razbirin, B.S.
Starukhin, A.N.
Eurov, D.A.
Kurdyukov, D.A.
Stovpiaga, E. Yu
Golubev, V.G.
coredata.dc:description: Photophysical properties of carbon dots were investigated under various excitation conditions and over a wide temperature region – from room to liquid helium temperatures. The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using mesoporous silica particles as a reactor and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) as a precursor. The photoluminescence spectra of CDs exhibit a strong dependence on the excitation wavelength and demonstrate a significant inhomogeneous broadening. Lowering sample temperature reveals the doublet structure of the spectra, which is associated with the vibronic structure of radiative transitions. The vibration energy ∼1200 cm−1 is close to the energy of CO stretching vibration. Long-lived phosphorescence of carbon dots with its decay time ∼0.2 s at T = 80 K was observed. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra are shown to be spectrally separated. The long-lived component of the emission was ascribed to optically forbidden triplet-singlet transitions. The value of the singlet-triplet splitting was found to be about 0.3 eV. Photo-induced polarization of the luminescence of carbon dots was revealed. The degree of the linear polarization is dependent on the wavelengths of both excitation and emitted light. The effect indicates a hidden anisotropy of optical dipole transitions in the dots and demonstrates the loss of the dipole orientation during the electron energy relaxation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Phosphorescence
Photo-induced polarization

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 5-th International Workshop on Advanced Spectroscopy and Optical Materials (IWASOM’15)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence spectra of CDs at room temperature. 1 – colloid solution of CDs, excitation wavelength 405 nm; 2 – CDs in mesoporous silica, excitation wavelength 405 nm; 3 – CDs in mesoporous silica, excitation wavelength 532 nm.
Photoluminescence spectra of CDs in mesoporous silica. T = 80 K. Excitation wavelength: 1–405 nm, 2–532 nm, 1–633 nm. Insert shows the same spectra, but normalized on the intensity.

Photoluminescence spectra of colloid solution of CDs. T = 80 K. Excitation wavelength: 1–405 nm, 2–473 nm, 3–532 nm. Solid lines – experimental spectra, dashed lines – calculation. Insert illustrates the model of calculation – solid line is the spectrum of one individual emitter, dashed line is the whole spectrum of the system.

Time dependence of phosphorescence intensity of colloid solution of carbon dots. T = 80 K. Excitation wavelength 405 nm. Intensity is measured at band maximum (λ = 540 nm). Straight line shows the exponential dependence I = I0exp(-t/τ) with decay time τ = 0.2 s.

Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of colloid solution of carbon dots. T = 2 K. Excitation wavelengths: a – 405 nm, b – 457 nm, c – 473 nm. Solid lines – fluorescence (1), measured synchronously with the excitation pulse; doted lines (2) – phosphorescence measured with time delay of 100 μs. Figure d shows the dependence of the energy difference between fluorescence and phosphorescence peaks on the position of fluorescence band maximum.
Scheme of the experiment on photo-induced polarization of luminescence.
Linear polarization degree of CDs luminescence plotted against emission wavelength. T = 300 K. Excitation wavelength: 1–405 nm, 2–473 nm, 3–532 nm.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence of carbon dots from mesoporous silica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ioffe Physical-Technical Institute, Russian Academy of Sciences, 194021, St. Petersburg, Russia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Ioffe Physical-Technical Institute, Polytekhnicheskaya 26, 194021, St. Petersburg, Russia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photophysical properties of carbon dots were investigated under various excitation conditions and over a wide temperature region – from room to liquid helium temperatures. The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using mesoporous silica particles as a reactor and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) as a precursor. The photoluminescence spectra of CDs exhibit a strong dependence on the excitation wavelength and demonstrate a significant inhomogeneous broadening. Lowering sample temperature reveals the doublet structure of the spectra, which is associated with the vibronic structure of radiative transitions. The vibration energy ∼1200 cm−1 is close to the energy of CO stretching vibration. Long-lived phosphorescence of carbon dots with its decay time ∼0.2 s at T = 80 K was observed. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra are shown to be spectrally separated. The long-lived component of the emission was ascribed to optically forbidden triplet-singlet transitions. The value of the singlet-triplet splitting was found to be about 0.3 eV. Photo-induced polarization of the luminescence of carbon dots was revealed. The degree of the linear polarization is dependent on the wavelengths of both excitation and emitted light. The effect indicates a hidden anisotropy of optical dipole transitions in the dots and demonstrates the loss of the dipole orientation during the electron energy relaxation.

ce:section.ce:para: So-called carbon nanodots (carbogenic dots, C-dots; hereafter CDs) recently synthesized from a variety of organic materials have attracted a great attention due to their remarkable fluorescent properties comparable to those of semiconductor quantum dots. CDs photoluminescence covers all visible region of the spectrum, the spectral position of the emission band being easily controlled via the excitation wavelength. CDs could compete with semiconductor quantum dots in many applications, especially in biological and medical ones having the advantage of their low toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost and chemical inertness. The “surfaces” of CDs can be functionalized by different functional groups to bring chemical (biological) characteristics of the CDs to the desired ones. The effective use of CDs in the applications invites detailed investigations of their optical properties and finding out feasible mechanisms of the photoluminescence. Despite of a number of papers devoted to the study of photophysical properties of CDs these mechanisms still remain unclear.

A various techniques have been proposed for synthesis of carbon dots which are generally classified into two categories – ‘‘top-down’’ and ‘‘bottom-up’’ [1]. The former involves breaking down larger carbon structures, such as graphite [2] or graphite oxide [3]. The ‘‘bottom-up’’ approach means synthesis of carbon dots from molecular precursors such as citrate, carbohydrates and polymer–silica nanocomposites through combustion or thermal treatments [4–6]. In the present work we studied the carbon dots prepared in spherical mesoporous silica particles whose pores was used as peculiar reactors [7]. This technique of carbon dots preparation was chosen because of remarkable properties of the particles. They have large specific surface area (>700 m2 g-1) and pore volume (>0.5 cm3 g-1). The mesostructure is chemically stable, the monodisperse cylindrical pores being 3 nm in diameter. The silica particles can be successfully used as a drug delivery vehicles [8]. Thus the carbon dots incorporated into the mesopores can play a role of fluorescent markers that facilitate the tracking of silica particles in a biological system [9]. The paper presents results of our studying photoluminescent properties of CDs synthesized in the silica matrices using (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) as precursor. The feasibility of this synthesis was demonstrated in Ref. [9]. The method used provides carbon dots with a diameter of ∼3 nm [9] with their carbon cores amorphous in the structure [10].

Monodisperse spherical mesoporous silica particles were obtained by the technique described elsewhere [11]. The diameter of the particles was 500 ± 20 nm. The average particle diameter and the standard deviation were calculated from the diameters of about 50 particles observed under a transmission electron microscope [12]. To synthesize carbon dots within the mesopores we used capillary impregnation technique. A weighed portion of the mesoporous silica spheres was impregnated with a 33% (by volume) solution of APTES in ethanol. Than, the solution was evaporated and the resulted particles were annealed at a temperature of 250 °C for 2 h. Thermal decomposition of APTES resulted in formation of carbon dots randomly distributed within the silica spheres. Dissolving the silica spheres in hydrofluoric acid releases the carbon dots from the matrix giving rise to a transparent colloid solution of the dots. In our experiments we used both types of samples – carbon dots in mesoporous silica in form of pale yellow powder and carbon dots colloid solution light brown in color.

The photoluminescence properties of matrix-isolated carbon dots have been investigated under different excitation conditions and over a wide range of temperatures from that of liquid helium (2 K) to room temperature (300 K). The luminescence was excited by the radiation of solid-state lasers with different emission wavelengths. The excitation density did not exceed 10 W/cm2. The samples under study were placed in quartz cuvettes of optical quality which were transparent in a wide frequency range from near UV to the IR. Registration of optical spectra was carried out using a double grating spectrometer and a photon counting system based on National Instruments M Series data acquisition device NI-6224. Kinetics of luminescence was measured using two techniques. The first technique was “Time correlated single photon counting” [13]. The second harmonic of pulsed YAG-Nd laser was used in this case as an excitation source (pulse duration 7 ns) and the time resolution was about 10 ns. To measure much slower luminescence kinetics, like phosphorescence, the second technique was used – “Time-resolved multi-channel photon counting”. In this case the excitation was provided by CW solid state laser with external modulation and the photons were put into channels during counting in correspondence with their arrival time. The best time resolution we have for this technique is 0.5 μs and this technique allows to measure long decay times about seconds or even more.

Room-temperature photoluminescence spectra of the carbon dots embedded into a silica matrix and of their colloidal solution are depictured in Fig. 1 . Under excitation with violet radiation (λexc = 405 nm) the spectra consist of broad asymmetrical bands with its maximum position slightly dependent on the dots environment. In case of the colloid solution the maximum is shifted to higher energy as compared with its position in the spectrum of the dots in mesoporous silica (Fig. 1). It is not clear now if the difference in the spectra is caused by dissimilarity of the environments or by the effect of hydrofluoric acid on the dots properties. Excitation by a radiation with longer wavelengths causes the narrowing of the emission band and shifting the band maximum to the longer wavelength side. Such behavior of the emission spectra is characteristic for inhomogeneously broadened emission of ensemble of emitters. This conclusion is consistent with the results of investigating the emission spectra of single carbon dots [14]. It is likely that the position of the emission band maximum in the spectrum of an individual carbon dot is determined mainly by its size: the larger the particle the longer the emission wavelength [15]. One can assume that this dependence holds true for CDs synthesized from APTES as well.

When studying the low-temperature photoluminescence of carbon dots it was found that after the exciting light was switched off a naked-eyed afterglow was observed. The long-lived emission is characterized by its wavelengths evidently longer than the fluorescence wavelengths. Thus, luminescence of the CDs contains two components – fast and slow. The measurements of decay time of fast component gave the value about 10 ns, but it lies on the resolution limit of our equipment. The results of the measurements the time dependence of phosphorescence intensity are presented in Fig. 4 . As it can be seen in the figure, the phosphorescence exhibits a single exponential decay with decay time of 0.2 s at liquid nitrogen temperature.

The long-lived component is especially intensive in the frozen colloid solution of carbon dots while it is weak (though observable) in case of carbon dots in silica matrix. The time-resolved spectroscopy revealed an essential difference in the emission band positions in the spectra of the fast and slow components. The corresponding spectra were registered under pulsed laser excitation and are presented in Fig. 5 . Here the spectra of fast component (fluorescence) were obtained synchronously with the excitation pulse, the spectra of slow component (phosphorescence) were taken with time delay of 100 μs. The spectra were measured at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm (Fig. 5a), 457 nm (b) and 473 nm (c).

The energy distance between fast and slow components depends on the excitation wavelength. It decreases with increase of excitation wavelength. The energy distance is linearly dependent on the fast component peak energy (see Fig. 5d). The slow component could be attributed to radiative decay of the triplet states of carbon dots. The radiative transitions from triplet exited states to singlet ground state are spin-forbidden, so that the decay time of triplet states could be very long, especially at low temperatures. Additionally, there is a belief that the emission of a carbon dot is due to electronic transitions in molecular organic complexes on the dot “surface” [17]. However, it is known that organic molecular phosphorescence is invariably a triplet-singlet intercombination [18]. At room temperature the phosphorescence attributed to radiative decay of triplet states was observed in PVA-passivated carbon dots [19] and in grapheme quantum dots [20]. Our results correlate well with the results [19,20]. The value of the singlet-triplet splitting is determined in particular by the size of a molecule under study [21]. Is this connection the decrease in the singlet-triplet splitting with increasing the excitation wavelength can be explained by larger contribution in the luminescence of large-sized CDs under conditions of longer wavelength excitation, which is consistent with the above-cited proposition.

Another remarkable feature of carbon dot luminescence is an effect of photo-induced polarization of the luminescence. The scheme of the experiment is shown in Fig. 6 . The sample is excited by light linearly polarized with E ||z. The photoluminescence is detected in two orthogonal polarizations E ||z and E ||y. The degree of the linear polarization of the emission can be determined as:
P L
=
I
| |
−
I ⊥
I
| |
+
I ⊥
where I || and I ⊥ are the emission intensities for light polarizations E ||z and E ||y respectively. The wavelength dependence of the polarization degree is presented in Fig. 7 for three excitation wavelengths. One can see that the CDs emission is remarkably polarized with the degree of polarization about 0.1–0.2. For short wavelength excitation (405 nm) the polarization degree is maximal on the short-wavelength side of the emission band and falls down to zero at the long wavelength side. For excitation with longer wavelengths (473 nm and 532 nm) the change of the polarization degree with the wavelength is far less: the polarization degree is approximately constant through the whole emission band. The maximum value of the polarization degree increases with increasing the excitation wavelength. The conservation of the linear polarization of the exciting light in CDs luminescence indicates that the orientations of the dipole moments of the transitions responsible for the photoexcitation and for the light emission are the same and are rigidly associated with an individual CD. This conclusion was strongly confirmed by the direct observation of luminous single carbon dots in polarized light [14]. The photo-induced optical anisotropy of CDs was also studied in Ref. [22]. However, the value of the steady-state polarization degree observed in Ref. [22] was several times less than in our case.

Fig. 7 demonstrates that for a given excitation wavelengths the polarization degree falls with increasing the emission wavelength. In other words, the polarization degree is evidently dependent on the number of intermediate electronic states which are involved in the process of energy relaxation of photocreated electronic excitations.

In the case of an ensemble of emiting objects randomly oriented in space and provided that transition dipole moments corresponding to absorption and emission are parallel, the degree of linear polarization can reach 50% [23]. The lower value of the maximum polarization degree observed under conditions of the long wavelength excitation (about 20%) can be explained by depolarization effect which is due to thermal rotation of the individual CDs in the liquid matrix. Lowering temperature of the colloidal CDs solution results in the increase of the polarization degree, which confirms the assumption.

Photophysical properties of carbon dots synthesized in mesoporous silica particles were investigated under different excitation conditions and over a wide temperature region. The photoluminescence response of the dots exhibits a strong dependence on the excitation wavelength. A significant inhomogeneous broadening of the emission spectra is established. The doublet structure of the low-temperature photoluminescence spectra was detected under long-wavelength excitation. This structure is associated with the vibronic nature of the radiative transitions. The vibration energy ∼1200 cm−1 is close to the energy of CO stretching vibration. Long-lived phosphorescence of carbon dots with decay time ∼0.2 s at T = 80 K was observed. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra are shown to be spectrally well separated. The long-lived component of the emission was ascribed to optically forbidden triplet-singlet transitions. The value of the singlet-triplet splitting was found to be about 0.3 eV. Photo-induced polarization of the luminescence of carbon dots was revealed. The degree of the linear polarization is dependent on the wavelengths of both excitation and emitted light. The effect indicates a hidden anisotropy of optical dipole transitions in the dots and demonstrates the loss of the dipole orientation during the electron energy relaxation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The reported study was funded by Russian Foundation for Basic Research according to the research project No. 16-03-00472а.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular Spectroscopy of the Triplet State
